name: John Kasich
subject: Honoring Dr. King's Legacy
body:
Last year, thanks to the invitation of Bernice King, I had the
privilege of celebrating the legacy of Martin Luther King Jr. at
the King Center in Atlanta. It was one of the great experiences
of my lifetime

Peter, last year, thanks to the
invitation of Bernice King, I had the privilege of celebrating
the legacy of Martin Luther King Jr. at the King Center in
Atlanta. It was one of the great experiences of my lifetime. It
was so moving to be in the house where Dr. King was raised and to
walk the street that he walked.

Today, at a time of such great
dissension, Dr. King's legacy is a reminder that regardless of
current political leaders, we can all make a difference. I
believe Dr. King was concerned about we are doing for our
neighbors, for the people who live next door.

What's really important is that
we all work to make a difference. Frankly, the difference that
each one of us makes to improve the lives of those around us can
change the arc of the universe. Each one of us can make a
difference and we must.

Dr. King was a preacher and a
minister. As a person of faith, I believe that we are here to
reflect the glory of God. When we help others and give them a
chance to realize the purpose of their destiny, we are reflecting
the glory of God the same way Dr. King did his entire lifetime.

-John

Paid for by Kasich
for America, not authorized by any candidate or candidate's
committee.
www.JohnKasich.com

name: Tim Kaine
subject: Dr. King and the way forward
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Kaine for Virginia
body:
Today we honor the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. I am about to join a community service at First Baptist Church in Norfolk, VA where we will reflect on the meaning of his life to the challenges we face today.

It's a disturbing time. White supremacists descended upon Charlottesville in August to bring bigotry and violence to our Commonwealth. And just last week, a President who so often emboldens the voices of division made disgusting comments about people from nations in Central America, the Caribbean and Africa.

Dr. King would not be surprised at the persistence of these attitudes, even at the highest level of government. But he would remind us that darkness cannot be vanquished by more darkness, nor hate by more hate. More light and more love is what we need.

Recently, Virginia Congressman Bobby Scott and I convinced Congress to establish a Commission to commemorate 400 years of contributions by Africans and African-Americans to modern America. 2019 will be the anniversary of captured African slaves being brought ashore at Point Comfort, Virginia, beginning the massive forced diaspora of Africans into the English colonies. The centuries since have been a time of unimaginable horror and tragedy and transcendent achievement. And telling this story is a way to add light and love to a toxic moment in the life of our nation.

The President's recent comments demonstrate -- yet again -- who he is. Now it is up to the rest of us to demonstrate who we are. Dr. King decried passivity above all else. Let 2018 be a year of action!

Tim Kaine

| | | Paid for by Kaine for Virginia
---
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com.
If you no longer wish to receive updates from our campaign, please [unsubscribe](http://salsa3.salsalabs.com/dia/track.jsp?v=2&c=tFsnoBb%2BE7U8xKRrKEv%2FJr7VxWbH7iDh).
---
[](http://www.salsalabs.com/?email)
clean_body: Today we honor the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. I am about to join a community service at First Baptist Church in Norfolk, VA where we will reflect on the meaning of his life to the challenges we face today. It's a disturbing time. White supremacists descended upon Charlottesville in August to bring bigotry and violence to our Commonwealth. And just last week, a President who so often emboldens the voices of division made disgusting comments about people from nations in Central America, the Caribbean and Africa. Dr. King would not be surprised at the persistence of these attitudes, even at the highest level of government. But he would remind us that darkness cannot be vanquished by more darkness, nor hate by more hate. More light and more love is what we need. Recently, Virginia Congressman Bobby Scott and I convinced Congress to establish a Commission to commemorate 400 years of contributions by Africans and African-Americans to modern America. 2019 will be the anniversary of captured African slaves being brought ashore at Point Comfort, Virginia, beginning the massive forced diaspora of Africans into the English colonies. The centuries since have been a time of unimaginable horror and tragedy and transcendent achievement. And telling this story is a way to add light and love to a toxic moment in the life of our nation. The President's recent comments demonstrate -- yet again -- who he is. Now it is up to the rest of us to demonstrate who we are. Dr. King decried passivity above all else. Let 2018 be a year of action! Tim Kaine | | | Paid for by Kaine for Virginia

name: Ronna McDaniel
subject: I can’t call this fake news
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Republican National Committee
body:

Friend,

That quote is from a New York Times article that talks about my job as chairwoman of YOUR Party.

The article makes one point absolutely clear:the RNC is 100% behind President Donald J. Trump.

Back in 2016, some donors threatened to stop funding our Party if we continued to support Donald Trump. But we REFUSED to turn our backs on the millions of Americans who voted for a new direction for our nation.

Since then we’ve been able to bring on hundreds of thousands of new donors because for the first time in decades, the forgotten voices of the Silent Majority were brought to the forefront of our country.

That’s why I’m committed to working in line with the President’s vision and agenda, not just because it’s the President’s, but because it’s what YOU -- the people -- chose.

Now with the critical 2018 elections approaching, I hope I can count on you to continue fighting for our WINNING agenda by making a grassroots contribution to the RNC.

name: Andrew Gillum
subject: Unfinished
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Andrew Gillum, Democrat, for Governor
body:
[](http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/m/fcb31eb/9e00690/4307e2de/2403e14c/2112048685/VEsH/ "https://andrewgillum.com/?utm_medium=email&utm_source=gillum&utm_content=1&utm_campaign=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_&source=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_")
Hello,

In 1965, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. marched from Selma to Montgomery. During that march to end injustices, he said, "...we are not about to turn around. We are on the move now. Yes, we are on the move and no wave of racism can stop us."

**Over fifty years later his fight continues.**

Within the last year it's clear that, while we have come a long way in the civil rights movement since Dr. King's march, we're still on the move now.

Many of us tell ourselves that a flag or a statue, regardless of how demeaning in its display, can't harm us or keep us from reaching our life goals. Our fear is that if we pause long enough to internalize their message, maybe we'd grow hopelessly frustrated by our seeming inability to change things.

Because this is not the battle I imagine for my children or yours. Nor do I want them to still be fighting for voting rights, or a criminal justice system that doesn't disproportionately lock people of color up for crimes committed at the same rates.

We should have conversations about race, racism, sexism, and all the other -isms, because if it sits unconscious, we'll allow it to continue to perpetuate. Which is why today is not a day off, but a day on.

To honor Dr. King, we must continue to help America move closer to its promise of liberty and justice for all, and we remember the struggles left unfinished. Together, we will rebuild this state and make freedom a part of everybody's story, no matter what they look like or where they come from.

Bring it home,

Andrew

Paid for by Andrew Gillum, Democrat, for Governor.

---
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is one of the most important tools we have to reach supporters like you, but you can let us know if you'd like to [receive fewer emails](http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/m/fcb31eb/9e00690/4307e2de/2403e14f/2112048685/VEsE/p/eyJKU1ZGVFVGSlRDVWwiOiJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSIsIkpTVmFTVkFsSlE9PSI6IiJ9/ "http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/s/receive-fewer-emails?source=email_footer&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=&utm_medium=email&utm_source=gillum&utm_content=2+-+receive+fewer+emails&utm_campaign=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_"). We'd hate to see you go, but if you need to do so, click here to [unsubscribe](http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/m/fcb31eb/9e00690/4307e2de/2403e14e/2112048685/VEsF/ "https://gillum.cp.bsd.net/page/unsubscribe/?utm_medium=email&utm_source=gillum&utm_content=3+-+unsubscribe&utm_campaign=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_&source=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_"). [You can support our campaign by making a donation.](http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/m/fcb31eb/9e00690/4307e2de/2403e141/2112048685/VEsC/ "https://secure.actblue.com/contribute/page/ag-footer?utm_medium=email&utm_source=gillum&utm_content=4+-+You+can+support+our+campaign+by+making+a&utm_campaign=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_&source=em18_18011k_MLK__gq_")

name: Jason Yates
subject: MLK’s message to people of faith
body:
| | The speech we often overlook
---
[ ](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.myfaithvotes.org%2Fmlk-i-have-a-dream%2F&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=1)
| Peter, Today we remember Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. Most of us have heard his “I Have a Dream” speech – now one of the most famous speeches in all of history – and we might have read his famous letter from a Birmingham Jail. His messages drove a movement advancing civil rights in our nation, but they were much more than that. I hope we do not fail to recognize the importance of his messages to each follower of Christ, which are equally relevant for us today. I recently read again Dr. King’s speech, “I’ve Been to the Mountaintop.” It was the last speech he gave before his life was taken the very next day. I was struck by the power of his words for us today:
* * *
_Something is happening in Memphis; something is happening in our world. And you know, if I were standing at the beginning of time, with the possibility of taking a kind of general and panoramic view of the whole of human history up to now, and the Almighty said to me, "Martin Luther King, which age would you like to live in?" I would take my mental flight by Egypt and I would watch God's children in their magnificent trek from the dark dungeons of Egypt through, or rather across the Red Sea, through the wilderness on toward the promised land. And in spite of its magnificence, I wouldn't stop there._ _I would move on by Greece and take my mind to Mount Olympus. And I would see Plato, Aristotle, Socrates, Euripides and Aristophanes assembled around the Parthenon. And I would watch them around the Parthenon as they discussed the great and eternal issues of reality. But I wouldn't stop there.

I would go on, even to the great heyday of the Roman Empire. And I would see developments around there, through various emperors and leaders. But I wouldn't stop there._ _I would even come up to the day of the Renaissance, and get a quick picture of all that the Renaissance did for the cultural and aesthetic life of man. But I wouldn't stop there._ _I would even go by the way that the man for whom I am named had his habitat. And I would watch Martin Luther as he tacked his ninety-five theses on the door at the church of Wittenberg. But I wouldn't stop there._ _I would come on up even to 1863, and watch a vacillating President by the name of Abraham Lincoln finally come to the conclusion that he had to sign the Emancipation Proclamation. But I wouldn't stop there.

I would even come up to the early thirties, and see a man grappling with the problems of the bankruptcy of his nation. And come with an eloquent cry that _ _we have nothing to fear but "fear itself."__But I wouldn't stop there._ _Strangely enough, I would turn to the Almighty, and say, "If you allow me to live just a few years in the second half of the 20th century, I will be happy."_ _Now that's a strange statement to make, because the world is all messed up. The nation is sick. Trouble is in the land; confusion all around. That's a strange statement. But I know, somehow, that only when it is dark enough can you see the stars. And I see God working in this period of the twentieth century in a way that men, in some strange way, are responding._ _Something is happening in our world. The masses of people are rising up. And wherever they are assembled today, whether they are in Johannesburg, South Africa; Nairobi, Kenya; Accra, Ghana; New York City; Atlanta, Georgia; Jackson, Mississippi; or Memphis, Tennessee -- the cry is always the same: "We want to be free."_[_Read more of the speech._](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.myfaithvotes.org%2Fmlk-i-have-a-dream%2F&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=2)
* * *
Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. was killed at just 39 years old, but he lived out God’s calling and purpose for his life - to make a mark on history right where God placed him. We should seek to live our lives knowing that God placed us in America in this moment in history for a reason and a purpose. Let us be faithful with the calling He has for us in our generation as Martin Luther King Jr. was with his. “You, my brothers and sisters, were called to be free. But do not use your freedom to indulge the flesh; rather, serve one another humbly in love. For the entire law is fulfilled in keeping this one command: ‘love your neighbor as yourself.’ - Galatians 5:13-14 **United. We Stand.**

Jason Yates
CEO
[](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.myfaithvotes.org%2F&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=3)
* * *
_My Faith Votes is a non-partisan movement focused on motivating Christians in America to participate in local and national elections and be active members of society. By partnering with local churches, pastors, and national faith leaders, My Faith Votes mobilizes and equips Christians to lead the conversation on the place of faith in culture and politics._
---
* * *
©2018 My Faith Votes
10940 S Parker, 640, Parker, CO 80134
This email was sent to [dpwillis67@gmail.com](dpwillis67@gmail.com).
To stop receiving emails, [click here](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/unsubscribe?e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=4).
[ ](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.facebook.com%2Fmyfaithvotes&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=5) [ ](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.twitter.com%2Fmyfaithvotes&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=6) [ ](http://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.instagram.com%2Fmyfaithvotes&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=180115_com_s1&n=7)

name: Nina Turner, Our Revolution
subject: The Real American Dream
body:
Dr. King's legacy is carried on in Our Revolution's struggle
#
Friends,
At the time of Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.'s assassination almost fifty years ago, he was deeply engaged in the fight against the triple evils of racism, militarism, and materialism. He was committed to building a movement to challenge the concentration of economic power at the top of our society. With the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act signed into law, Dr. King traveled to Memphis to support municipal sanitation workers who were striking after two of their members, Echol Cole and Robert Walker, were killed by garbage compactors at work. His belief that we would one day achieve the beloved community was expressed so eloquently the night before his death in the speech, "I've Been to the Mountaintop."
Dr. King's legacy is carried on in Our Revolution's struggle to realize racial, social, economic, and environmental justice across America. Like Dr. King, we are working to build a society that does not wage unjust wars, whether in Vietnam or Iraq. Dr. King's Dream is about reforming our economic system so that a child's future is not predetermined by their parents' wealth, education, or zip code. It is about eradicating the institutional barriers that have kept people of color and those who speak other languages from the prosperous livelihood we call the American Dream. We march, speak, organize, work, and vote to bring Dr. King's ideas to life because his Dream is the true American Dream.
[**Please share your story of what Martin Luther King, Jr. Day means to you, and help spread Dr. King's message of love and prosperity to carry on his legacy.**](https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/m/2ba7adb4/bed43eb/35c53a14/75fead09/723141631/VEsH/ "https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/s/mlk-day?source=em180115-full")
Dr. King would be proud of the coalition our movement is building because, despite the divisions in our society, the vast majority of people recognize the need for respect, compassion, and justice, and we are working to accomplish just that. We still live in a country where sixty years ago a person could be arrested for drinking from the wrong water fountain, and today their grandchild could be arrested, or worse, for driving with a broken taillight. Friends, there is so much work left to be done. We must continue to work together in order to finish the work that Dr. King started, and that people in power have resisted since well before his work began.
[**Share what Martin Luther King, Jr. Day means to you and help carry on his legacy of love, equality, and justice for all.**](https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/m/2ba7adb4/bed43eb/35c53a14/75fead09/723141631/VEsE/ "https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/s/mlk-day?source=em180115-full")
No matter the challenges that each day presents, we can overcome them to build an America as great as its promise, and finally attain the true measure of justice that is the birthright of every human being.
In solidarity,
Nina Turner
President
Our Revolution
**[SHARE YOUR STORY»](https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/m/2ba7adb4/bed43eb/35c53a14/75fead09/723141631/VEsF/ "https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/s/mlk-day?source=em180115-full")**
PAID FOR BY OUR REVOLUTION
603 2ND ST, NE
WASHINGTON, DC, 20002

Not authorized by any candidate or candidate committee. Our Revolution is a 501(c)(4) organization. Donations to Our Revolution are not deductible as charitable contributions for Federal income tax purposes. All donations are made to support Our Revolution's general mission and are not designated for any specific activity.
603 2nd St NE, Washington, DC 20002

Help us continue transforming American politics and fighting for progressive candidates and causes by [contributing to Our Revolution here](https://go.ourrevolution.com/page/m/2ba7adb4/bed43eb/35c53a14/75fead35/723141631/VEsB/ "https://secure.actblue.com/contribute/page/ourrevolution?refcode=email-footer-OR").
clean_body: Dr. King's legacy is carried on in Our Revolution's struggle Friends, At the time of Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.'s assassination almost fifty years ago, he was deeply engaged in the fight against the triple evils of racism, militarism, and materialism. He was committed to building a movement to challenge the concentration of economic power at the top of our society. With the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act signed into law, Dr. King traveled to Memphis to support municipal sanitation workers who were striking after two of their members, Echol Cole and Robert Walker, were killed by garbage compactors at work. His belief that we would one day achieve the beloved community was expressed so eloquently the night before his death in the speech, "I've Been to the Mountaintop." Dr. King's legacy is carried on in Our Revolution's struggle to realize racial, social, economic, and environmental justice across America. Like Dr. King, we are working to build a society that does not wage unjust wars, whether in Vietnam or Iraq. Dr. King's Dream is about reforming our economic system so that a child's future is not predetermined by their parents' wealth, education, or zip code. It is about eradicating the institutional barriers that have kept people of color and those who speak other languages from the prosperous livelihood we call the American Dream. We march, speak, organize, work, and vote to bring Dr. King's ideas to life because his Dream is the true American Dream. "" Dr. King would be proud of the coalition our movement is building because, despite the divisions in our society, the vast majority of people recognize the need for respect, compassion, and justice, and we are working to accomplish just that. We still live in a country where sixty years ago a person could be arrested for drinking from the wrong water fountain, and today their grandchild could be arrested, or worse, for driving with a broken taillight. Friends, there is so much work left to be done. We must continue to work together in order to finish the work that Dr. King started, and that people in power have resisted since well before his work began. "" No matter the challenges that each day presents, we can overcome them to build an America as great as its promise, and finally attain the true measure of justice that is the birthright of every human being. In solidarity, Nina Turner President Our Revolution "" PAID FOR BY OUR REVOLUTION 603 2ND ST, NE WASHINGTON, DC, 20002 Not authorized by any candidate or candidate committee. Our Revolution is a 501(c)(4) organization. Donations to Our Revolution are not deductible as charitable contributions for Federal income tax purposes. All donations are made to support Our Revolution's general mission and are not designated for any specific activity. 603 2nd St NE, Washington, DC 20002

name: Donna Edwards
subject: We're still fighting
body:
[](http://action.donnaforprincegeorges.org/page/m/74feff66/767134fb/5ce098d7/433810f1/989944977/VEsH/ "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/dfe-email?refcode=em180112")
Friend,
A lot has changed in the 54 years since Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. made his "I Have a Dream" speech, but in many ways, we're still fighting to make Dr. King's dream a reality.
We're still fighting to make sure all that kids receive a quality education, that every worker gets a fair wage, and that no person in this country feels marginalized because of who they are or who they love.
One of my favorite quotes from Dr. King is this: "Life's most persistent and urgent question is, 'What are you doing for others?'" \-- and I feel inspired and challenged by his words.
We can all play a part in continuing Dr. King's work and fulfilling his dream. That's why today is so special -- it's a reminder for us to get out and serve our community. I know I will be doing my part and volunteering at the Hyattsville Day of Service.
The work we do ensures we're building an America that lives up to its creed: all people are created equal. I hope you'll join me in continuing Dr. King's legacy,
Donna

This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com.
Email is the best way to stay in contact with supporters like you, but if you wish to unsubscribe from all emails, please [click here.](http://action.donnaforprincegeorges.org/page/m/74feff66/767134fb/5ce098d7/43382f0e/989944977/VEsE/ "http://action.donnaforprincegeorges.org/unsubscribe")

Authority: Donna for Prince George's, Glenn Ivey, Treasurer
P.O. Box 44343
Fort Washington, MD 20749

************
